text: I find it fascinating that trees can communicate with each other underground through what scientists call the "wood wide web" — a network of fungal threads called mycorrhizal networks that connect the roots of different trees. When one tree is stressed or damaged, it can send chemical signals through these networks to warn neighboring trees, which then increase their defenses against pests or prepare for drought. Even more intriguingly, trees can transfer nutrients to each other through these networks, essentially sharing resources with their neighbors or even their own offspring. A mother tree can recognize her seedlings and preferentially funnel them extra carbon and nutrients to help them survive in the shade of the forest canopy. It completely challenges our intuition that trees are just solitary, competing organisms. They're actually engaged in what looks remarkably like cooperation and communication, which raises all sorts of interesting questions about plant cognition and whether we should think about forests more as superorganisms than collections of individuals.
